# wobbly v0.2.0

Parallelize `map`, `filter`, `reduce`, and `forEach` over large arrays using Web Workers.
No worker file to build or serve. ~2kB gzipped, zero dependencies.

- Source: https://github.com/tonioloewald/wobbly
- npm: https://www.npmjs.com/package/wobbly-js (the package is `wobbly-js`; `wobbly` is taken)

There is no doc site; this file and the README are the documentation. It is hand-maintained,
not generated — if you change the API, change this too.

## The two things you will get wrong

### 1. Callbacks have no closure

Callbacks are serialized with `Function.prototype.toString()` and rebuilt inside the worker with
`new Function()`. They arrive with **no closure**. Referencing anything from the enclosing scope
throws `ReferenceError` inside the worker.

```js
const offset = 3
const helper = (n) => n * 2
await asyncNums.map((n) => helper(n) + offset) // ✗ ReferenceError in the worker
```

Pass what the callback needs to `withContext()`; it becomes the callback's `this`. Use a
`function`, not an arrow — arrows cannot be re-bound.

```js
function addOffset(n) {
  return n + this.offset
} // ✓
await asyncNums.withContext({ offset: 3 }).map(addOffset)
```

Context must survive `JSON.stringify` (plain data only). Items must survive structured clone.
If a callback needs a helper, inline it into the callback body.

### 2. A parallel `reduce` is not a serial `reduce`

The array is folded in chunks and the partials are merged, so an un-`combine`d reducer must be
**associative**. A non-associative one returns a confidently wrong answer with no error:

```js
;[10, 1, 2, 3].reduce((a, n) => a - n) // 4
await new AsyncArray([10, 1, 2, 3]).reduce((a, n) => a - n) // NOT 4
```

Whenever the accumulator is a different shape from the items, folding an item and merging two
accumulators are different operations — say so with `combine`:

```js
await new AsyncArray(harvest).reduce(
  (counts = {}, item) => {
    counts[item.fruit] = (counts[item.fruit] || 0) + 1
    return counts
  },
  {
    combine: (a, b) => {
      for (const [k, n] of Object.entries(b)) a[k] = (a[k] || 0) + n
      return a
    },
  }
)
```

`combine` runs on the **main thread**, so — unlike every other callback — it has a normal closure.
There is no seed: the accumulator arrives `undefined` on the first item, so give it a default.
Reducing an empty array throws, as `Array.reduce` does.

Note floating-point addition is not truly associative, so a parallel sum is not bit-identical to
a serial one (~1e-9 relative). Don't parallelize a reduce that must be bit-exact.

## API

```js
import { AsyncArray } from 'wobbly-js'
```

`new AsyncArray(array, options?)` — `options: { workers?: number }`

| Method | Resolves to |
| --- | --- |
| `map(fn, options?)` | new array of results, in input order |
| `filter(fn, options?)` | matching items, in input order |
| `reduce(fn, options?)` | the reduced value (see above) |
| `forEach(fn, options?)` | `undefined` |
| `withContext(obj)` | a **new** `AsyncArray` binding `obj` as the callback's `this` |
| `withWorkers(n)` | a **new** `AsyncArray` claiming `n` workers per operation |

`options` is `{ onProgress?, signal? }` (plus `combine?` for `reduce`), or a bare function as
shorthand for `{ onProgress }`. `withContext` and `withWorkers` do not mutate the receiver.

Module functions: `configureWorkerPool({ size })` (before first use) and `terminateWorkerPool()`.
Types: `WobblyContext`, `WobblyCallback`, `WobblyReducer`, `WobblyCombiner`, `OperationOptions`,
`ReduceOptions`, `AsyncArrayOptions`.

### Progress

Automatic — the callback does not cooperate. `this.progress()` still exists as a deprecated no-op.

```js
await asyncNums.map(fn, (progress) => (bar.value = progress)) // 0…1
```

### Cancellation

```js
const controller = new AbortController()
const pending = asyncNums.filter(fn, { signal: controller.signal })
controller.abort() // pending rejects with signal.reason (AbortError)
```

A JS hot loop cannot be interrupted, so aborting **terminates** the workers and replaces them.
The work really stops; side effects already performed inside those workers stand.

### Errors

A callback that throws in a worker **rejects** the promise; the workers are replaced.

## When to use it — and when not to

Only worth it when per-item work is heavy enough to pay for copying the data in and out.
Measured on 10 cores (an operation claims half the pool by default, so ~5 workers):

- Primality of 100k integers near 1e12: 3562ms → 757ms (**4.7× faster**)
- Trivial predicate over 10M random floats: 20ms → 211ms (**11× slower**)

A cheap callback over a huge array is the worst case. Reach for wobbly when a pass is janking the
main thread, not merely because an array is big.

## Limits

- **Needs `unsafe-eval`** (`new Function()`) and `worker-src blob:`. Will not run under a strict
  CSP; no Chrome MV3 extensions. This is the deliberate price of having no worker file to build
  or serve.
- **Browser or Bun only.** Node has `worker_threads`, not the web `Worker` global.
- `forEach` runs in a worker with no DOM and no access to main-thread state.
- The whole array is copied (structured clone), so peak memory is roughly double. No transferables
  or TypedArray fast path yet.
